title: Why Tests Matter
intro: You just joined a small startup. The codebase has zero tests. Imagine walking a tightrope without a safety net -- one slip and it's a disaster. Your first task is to add a new payment feature. After you ship it, things start going wrong. Let's see why tests would have saved the day.

teach.title: Tests = Safety Net for a Tightrope Walker
teach.body: A tightrope walker CAN perform without a safety net. But one slip and it's a disaster. A safety net doesn't help you walk better -- it catches you when you fall. **Tests** are your safety net. They don't write your code for you, but they catch you when a change accidentally breaks something.

teach.title: Tests = Smoke Detectors in Your House
teach.body: You don't install smoke detectors because you expect a fire every day. You install them because the ONE time something goes wrong, they save everything. **Tests** are the smoke detectors of your codebase. They sit quietly in the background, and the moment something catches fire -- a broken feature, a bad calculation -- they scream loudly before the damage spreads.

teach.title: The Real Reasons Tests Matter
teach.body:
Tests save you time and stress in four big ways:

1. **Catch bugs early** -- Find problems in 2 seconds instead of discovering them from angry users
2. **Change code fearlessly** -- Refactor knowing tests will tell you if you broke something
3. **Document behavior** -- Tests show exactly what your code is supposed to do
4. **Sleep better** -- Deploy on Friday knowing your tests passed

teach.title: The Cost of NOT Testing
teach.body: Without tests, every change is risky. You have to manually check everything. Did the login still work? Does checkout still process payments? Did the search still return results? This manual checking takes time, and you'll inevitably miss something. One missed bug in a payment system can cost real money.

Every bug that reaches your users is a refund request, a bad review, or a lost customer. When you ship AI-generated apps, tests are the difference between a side project that makes money and one that destroys your reputation overnight.
Tests catch bugs early, let you change code confidently, document expected behavior, and prevent regressions. The time you spend writing tests saves much more time debugging later.

start.narrative: You added a discount code feature to checkout. It worked locally. You deployed Thursday afternoon. Friday morning, a panicked message: 'Customers are getting 100% discounts on all orders!' What's your first move?

choices.text: Open the discount calculation code and look for the bug
Immediately disable the discount feature in production
Check if someone entered a fraudulent discount code
A test asserting applyDiscount(100, 20) === 80 would have caught this in 2 seconds
Tests wouldn't have helped -- this is a logic error

disable-feature.narrative: Smart -- you stopped the bleeding. Now you trace the bug: applyDiscount(100, 20) returns -1900 instead of 80. The function multiplies by 20 instead of 0.20. What's the lesson here?

choices.text: Even one test for this function would have caught it before deploy
Just ship the fix -- tests are nice but slow you down

check-fraud.narrative: It's not fraud -- the codes are legitimate. The math is wrong. Even valid 20% codes produce negative totals because the function multiplies by 20 instead of 0.20.

write-the-tests.narrative: You sit down to write tests for the fixed discount function. What cases should you cover beyond the obvious 20% case?

choices.text: Boundary values: 0%, 100%, negative percent, and very large prices
Just the cases that match what the buggy code produced
One happy-path test -- tests for edge cases are overkill

test-related-code.narrative: The discount function is now safe. But discount codes touch tax calculation, totals, and the receipt rendering. None of those have tests either. How do you decide what to cover next?

choices.text: Cover everything that touches money: tax, totals, receipts, refund flow
Add tests one-by-one whenever a related bug is reported
Stop here -- you've fixed the immediate problem

prevent-recurrence.narrative: You've shipped the fix and added tests. Final question: how do you make sure no future change reintroduces this exact bug?

choices.text: Block merges in CI when discount tests fail and require a test for any new pricing code
Add a comment in the code saying 'remember to divide by 100'
Trust the team to remember this incident

end-good.narrative: You shipped the fix, wrote boundary-aware tests for discount, extended coverage to every money-touching path, and locked it in with a CI rule. The same bug class can never silently ship again, and the next contributor inherits the constraint without needing to know your story.
end-good.feedback: This is the full arc: investigate, fix, harden the local code with strong tests, expand coverage to neighbors, and enforce it in CI. One incident becomes a permanent guarantee.

end-okay.narrative: The fix shipped. The discount function is tested. But pricing-related code outside the discount file has no enforcement, and a comment is the weakest possible safeguard -- the next refactor will delete it.
end-okay.feedback: You handled the immediate fire well. The miss is enforcement: comments aren't tests, and tests that don't run in CI are barely tests. Lock the fix in with automation.

end-poor.narrative: You fixed the function, but didn't add tests, didn't expand coverage, and rely on the team's memory. In six months a new hire will refactor the discount logic and the same bug will return -- because nothing on disk prevents it.
end-poor.feedback: Memory is not a safety net. Without tests, the bug exists in latent form, waiting for the next change. The 30 seconds it takes to write the assertion is what stops the cycle.
